title: Embeddings make meaning searchable
text: Embeddings turn words, sentences, or chunks into vectors so that texts with similar meaning end up close together. That is why semantic search can match 'car' with 'vehicle' even when the exact words differ.

title: Tokenization before embedding
text: Before text becomes an embedding, the model usually breaks it into tokens. Tokenization helps the model process text as numbers, because neural networks work on token ids rather than raw strings.

title: Cosine similarity intuition
text: Cosine similarity measures whether two vectors point in a similar direction. For embeddings, a higher cosine similarity usually means the two texts are more semantically related.

title: OpenAI style embedding APIs
text: Many teams use embedding APIs from providers such as OpenAI or Cohere to convert text into vectors. The API returns a numeric representation that can be stored, indexed, and searched later.

title: SentenceTransformer models
text: Open-source embedding models such as all-MiniLM-L6-v2 provide a local way to create dense text vectors. They are useful when you want semantic search without sending every document to a hosted API.

title: FAISS is a vector index
text: FAISS stores vectors in an index so you can search by similarity instead of scanning every document one by one. It is fast and useful for nearest-neighbor search over embedding spaces.

title: ChromaDB stores embeddings and metadata
text: ChromaDB is a local-first vector store that keeps embeddings, source documents, and metadata together. You can add documents, query by text, and filter results with structured fields.

title: Chunking improves retrieval
text: Long documents are often split into smaller chunks before embedding. Chunking helps the retriever find the most relevant passage instead of forcing one long vector to represent everything.

title: Chunk overlap preserves context
text: A small overlap between chunks keeps related sentences from being separated too aggressively. Overlap is helpful when an important idea continues across a chunk boundary.

title: Top-k retrieval
text: Top-k retrieval returns the k most similar chunks for a query. It is simple, fast, and common in semantic search demos and RAG pipelines.

title: Similarity thresholding
text: Similarity thresholding keeps only documents that are above a minimum score. This reduces noisy matches, but if the threshold is too strict the system may return no results.

title: Query rewriting
text: Query rewriting changes the search text to better match the retrieval system. It can expand synonyms, remove unnecessary words, or restructure a complex question for better recall and precision.

title: RAG combines retrieval and generation
text: Retrieval-augmented generation first retrieves relevant context and then gives that context to the language model. This helps ground the answer in source material instead of relying only on memory.

title: Grounding reduces hallucination
text: Grounding means the model is supported by external evidence such as retrieved documents, a database, or a search index. It reduces hallucinations by tying the answer to source text.

title: Hallucination is unsupported output
text: Hallucination happens when a model gives a confident answer that is not supported by the facts. In RAG systems this risk is lower because the model can read the retrieved context first.

title: Mini-RAG pipeline steps
text: A mini-RAG pipeline usually follows three steps: retrieve the top chunks, augment the prompt with those chunks, and generate the response. The workflow is simple but powerful.

title: Bi-encoder and cross-encoder
text: A bi-encoder creates embeddings once and reuses them for fast search. A cross-encoder compares query and document together for better ranking quality, but it is slower.

title: Recall and precision in search
text: Recall measures how many relevant items you found, while precision measures how many returned items are actually relevant. Search systems often trade one for the other.

title: Mean reciprocal rank
text: Mean reciprocal rank looks at the position of the first relevant result. It is useful when the user usually cares about the first good answer more than the full ranking.

title: NDCG supports graded relevance
text: Normalized discounted cumulative gain gives more value to highly relevant results that appear near the top of the ranking. It is a strong metric when result order matters.

title: Weather and seasons
text: The Earth revolves around the Sun in about 365 days. This motion helps explain why the seasons change during the year.

title: Water boils at a standard point
text: At standard atmospheric pressure, water boils at 100 degrees Celsius. This is a basic physical fact often used in introductory science examples.

title: Paris is a major city
text: Paris is the capital city of France and is known for landmarks such as the Eiffel Tower. It is also a major center for art, fashion, and tourism.

title: Football is played worldwide
text: Football, also called soccer in some countries, is played by two teams trying to score goals. It is one of the most popular sports in the world.

title: The Taj Mahal is a landmark
text: The Taj Mahal is a famous monument in India built as a symbol of love. It is admired for its architecture and historical significance.
